title: Thyroid Physiology and Laboratory Evaluation
summary: Core physiology of the thyroid gland, synthesis of thyroid hormones (T3/T4), laboratory test interpretations, autoantibody panels, and nonisotopic thyroid imaging modalities.

subTopics.title: Regulation of Thyroid Function, Synthesis, and Function of Thyroid Hormones
subTopics.summary: Core physiology of the thyroid gland, detailing the neuroendocrine Regulation of the Hypothalamus-Pituitary-Thyroid (HPT) axis, biochemical synthesis of thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3), and molecular deiodinative regulation in target organs.
indications: • HPT Axis Kinetics: Pulsatile hypothalamic TRH stimulates anterior pituitary thyrotrophs via calcium-dependent GPCR pathways to secrete TSH. Circulating free T4 and T3 exert negative feedback on both pituitary TSH beta-subunit expression and hypothalamic TRH gene transcription.

• Active Hormone Synthesis: Follicular thyrocytes trap inorganic iodide via the basolateral Sodium-Iodide Symporter (NIS), driven by a Na+/K+ ATPase gradient. Iodide is transported across the apical membrane via Pendrin (SLC26A4) into the colloid cavity.

• TPO Organification: Apical Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) utilizes hydrogen peroxide generated by DUOX2 to oxidize iodide and organify it onto tyrosine residues of Thyroglobulin (Tg), generating monoiodotyrosine (MIT) and diiodotyrosine (DIT).

• Hormone Coupling & Secretion: TPO couples MIT and DIT to synthesize T3, and two DIT molecules to synthesize T4. Colloid endocytosis is followed by lysosomal proteolysis of Tg, releasing T4 and T3 into the bloodstream via the MCT8 and MCT10 transporters.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Non-Invasive Diagnostic Testing: Standard peripheral blood sampling and physiological testing require no anesthesia or sedation.
subTopics.positioning: • Palpation and Blood Draw: Comfortably seated or recumbent posture for venous access or clinical examination.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroid Follicle Ultrastructure: Single-layered cuboidal follicular epithelium enclosing colloid-filled follicular lumen, in close proximity to fenestrated capillaries.

techniqueSteps.text: • HPT Axis Feedback Loops: Central neuroendocrine axis demonstrating how hypothalamic TRH coordinates pituitary TSH secretion, and how free T3/T4 inhibit synthesis.
• Iodide Trapping and Transport: Basolateral NIS active transport of iodide coupled with sodium, followed by pendrin-mediated apical effusion into the follicle lumen.
• TPO-Catalyzed Organification: Apical oxidation of iodide and coupling on the thyroglobulin protein backbone, yielding iodotyrosyl residues.
• Tg Proteolysis & Transporter Efflux: Lysosomal degradation of endocytosed thyroglobulin colloid, with subsequent release of free hormones via MCT8 channels.
• Deiodinase Metabolic Pathways: Tissue-specific conversion of T4 to active T3 (D1, D2) vs. inactivation to rT3 (D3) in peripheral tissues.
• Nuclear Receptor Transcriptional Control: Binding of active T3 to nuclear thyroid hormone receptors (TR-alpha, TR-beta) leading to RXR heterodimerization and gene transcription.
• Monocarboxylate Transporter (MCT) Channels: Crucial membrane transporters that mediate thyroid hormone entry into target cells, particularly within the blood-brain barrier.

redZone.name: Wolff-Chaikoff Effect and Autoregulation
redZone.caution: • Acute Iodine Overload: Administering high iodide concentrations transiently inhibits TPO organification, a protective mechanism known as the Wolff-Chaikoff effect. In patients with latent autonomous nodules or Graves' disease, failure of this autoregulation can precipitate severe hyperthyroidism (Jod-Basedow effect).

subTopics.closure: • Metabolic Clearance: Peripheral 5'-deiodinases D1 and D2 convert inactive T4 to active T3, while 5-deiodinase D3 converts T4/T3 to inactive reverse T3 (rT3). Clearance occurs primarily via hepatic conjugation and biliary excretion.
subTopics.extensions: • Hormone Transport binding: Circulating hormones are 99.9% bound to thyroid-binding globulin (TBG), transthyretin (TTR), and albumin. Alterations in these proteins change total hormone levels but preserve physiologically active free hormone fractions.

subTopics.title: Tests of Thyroid Function
subTopics.summary: Surgical and clinical chemistry guidelines for utilizing serum TSH, free thyroid hormones, and radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) scintigraphy in patient evaluation.
indications: • TSH Sensitivity: Third-generation immunometric TSH assays represent the single most sensitive screening test for detecting primary thyroid dysfunction. Log-linear relationships exist between TSH and free T4.
• Free Hormone Indexes: Free T4 and Free T3 measurements are critical when total hormone levels are altered by TBG fluctuations (e.g., pregnancy, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, oral contraceptives).

• Functional Scintigraphy: Radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) using I-123 differentiates high-uptake hyperthyroidism (Graves', toxic MNG, toxic adenoma) from low-uptake thyrotoxicosis (subacute, postpartum, or factitious thyroiditis).
subTopics.anesthesia: • Outpatient Diagnostic Tests: Standard phlebotomy and nuclear scintigraphy require no anesthesia or local sedation.
subTopics.positioning: • Imaging Scans: Supine position under the gamma camera with the neck slightly extended to maximize visualization of thyroid lobes.
subTopics.landmarks: • Neck Anatomy Landmarks: Thyroid notch, cricoid cartilage, suprasternal notch, and anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

techniqueSteps.text: • Thyroid Function Test Algorithm: Initial TSH screening followed by reflex Free T4 testing to categorize overt or subclinical hyper- and hypothyroidism.
• RAIU Scintigraphy Scans: Diagnostic uptake mapping showing homogeneous high uptake (Graves') vs. patchy nodules (MNG) or absent uptake (thyroiditis).
• TBG Interference Profiles: Analysis of conditions that elevate (estrogen, hepatitis) or depress (androgens, steroids) TBG, causing false total hormone elevations.

redZone.name: Biotin Interference in Assays
redZone.caution: • Immunoassay Failure: Megadoses of Biotin (Vitamin B7) compete with biotinylated reagents in streptavidin-biotin sandwich assays, causing falsely low TSH and falsely high Free T4/T3, mimicking Graves' disease. Discontinue biotin for at least 72 hours before testing.

subTopics.closure: • Diagnostic Resolution: Check for discordance between TSH and free hormones, which may indicate central hypothyroidism, assay interference, or thyroid hormone resistance syndromes.
subTopics.extensions: • Euthyroid sick state: Critically ill patients show transient alterations in thyroid function tests (low T3, normal/low T4, high rT3, normal/low TSH) without intrinsic gland pathology. Re-evaluation post-recovery is mandatory.

subTopics.title: Thyroid Autoantibodies
subTopics.summary: Clinical utility of TPO, Tg, and TSH-receptor autoantibodies in diagnosing autoimmune thyroiditis and managing differentiated thyroid cancer surveillance.
indications: • Anti-TPO Antibodies: Present in 90-95% of patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis and 75-80% of Graves' disease. Predictive of progression from subclinical to overt hypothyroidism.
• Anti-Tg Antibodies: Frequently elevated in autoimmune thyroiditis. Measured concurrently with Thyroglobulin (Tg) in thyroid cancer surveillance to detect assay interference.

• TSH Receptor Antibodies (TRAb/TSI): TSI directly stimulates the TSH receptor, causing hyperthyroidism and thyroid growth in Graves' disease. Measured to confirm atypical Graves' or predict neonatal hyperthyroidism.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Venipuncture: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Blood Collection: Seated or supine position for outpatient phlebotomy.
subTopics.landmarks: • Autoimmune Lymphocytic Infiltration: Histological germinal center formation and follicular cell destruction within the thyroid parenchyma.

techniqueSteps.text: • Autoimmune Pathogenesis: Flowchart detailing T-cell mediated destruction in Hashimoto's vs. antibody-mediated receptor stimulation in Graves'.

redZone.name: TgAb Interference with Cancer Markers
redZone.caution: • False Negative Thyroglobulin: The presence of anti-Tg antibodies can cause false-negative thyroglobulin (Tg) results in immunometric sandwich assays. Always measure TgAb concurrently with Tg to avoid missing cancer recurrence.

subTopics.closure: • Assay Resolution: In cases of high TgAb, switch to radioimmunoassay (RIA) platforms or mass spectrometry to obtain accurate Tg measurements.
subTopics.extensions: • Maternal Antibody Transfer: High maternal TSI titers can cross the placenta in the third trimester, predicting a risk of neonatal thyrotoxicosis. Monitor TSI in pregnant Graves' patients.

subTopics.title: Nonisotopic Thyroid Imaging
subTopics.summary: Nonisotopic imaging modalities in the evaluation of thyroid pathology. Focus on high-resolution neck ultrasound (US) for nodule risk stratification and CT/MRI for compressive goiters.
indications: • High-Resolution Ultrasound: The gold standard for characterizing thyroid nodules, assessing malignancy risk (ACR TI-RADS), and directing fine-needle aspiration (FNA) biopsies.

• CT & MRI Scan Indications: Indicated for planning resection of retrosternal goiters, evaluating tracheal compression, and staging locally invasive thyroid cancers with adjacent structure encasement.
• Lymph Node Mapping: Systematic ultrasound evaluation of the central (Level VI) and lateral (Levels II-V) compartments to identify metastatic nodes prior to thyroidectomy.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Imaging Scans: Standard scans require no anesthesia. Claustrophobic patients may require mild sedation for MRI.
subTopics.positioning: • Hyperextended Neck Position: Supine position with a bolster behind the shoulders and head tilted back to maximize anatomical exposure of the neck.
subTopics.landmarks: • Vascular and Airway Landmarks: Trachea, esophagus, carotid arteries, internal jugular veins, strap muscles, omohyoid, and longus colli muscle.

techniqueSteps.text: • Nodule Ultrasound Risk Classification: ACR TI-RADS scoring system mapping features like microcalcifications and irregular margins to malignancy risk.
• Cross-Sectional Neck Anatomy: Spatial relationships of the thyroid lobes to the carotid sheath, trachea, and esophagus.
• Metastatic Lymph Node Criteria: Features of malignant cervical nodes including loss of fatty hilum, rounded shape, cystic change, and microcalcifications.

redZone.name: Contrast-Induced Iodine Overload
redZone.caution: • RAI Delay: Contrast-enhanced CT scans using iodinated contrast load the thyroid with iodine, preventing diagnostic radioactive iodine scans or therapeutic radioiodine ablation for 2-3 months. Avoid contrast if RAI is planned.

subTopics.closure: • TI-RADS Classification: Standardized reporting using ACR TI-RADS (composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, echogenic foci) to guide FNA decisions.
subTopics.extensions: • Postoperative Surveillance: Annual neck ultrasound is the cornerstone of follow-up for thyroid cancer surveillance to detect local recurrence.

title: Goiter and Thyroid Nodule
summary: Pathophysiology, diagnostics, and management of nontoxic goiter and thyroid nodules. Includes ultrasound risk stratification (TI-RADS) and fine-needle aspiration (FNA) decision algorithms.

subTopics.title: Nontoxic Goiter
subTopics.summary: Pathophysiology and surgical management of diffuse and multinodular non-toxic goiters, including compressive symptoms and retrosternal extension.
indications: • Compressive Symptoms: Dyspnea (worse with neck flexion), dysphagia, globus sensation, and voice changes from displacement or compression of the trachea/esophagus.
• Pemberton's Sign: Facial congestion, cyanosis, and jugular venous distension when both arms are raised above the head, indicating thoracic inlet obstruction by a retrosternal goiter.
• Cosmetic and Growth Indications: Progressive goiter enlargement causing neck deformity or trachea displacement even in the absence of severe symptoms.
subTopics.anesthesia: • General Anesthesia: Inhalation induction with endotracheal intubation. Secure the airway carefully, as a compromised airway or tracheal deviation may require fiberoptic intubation.
subTopics.positioning: • Rosser Position: Supine position with head of table elevated 15 degrees to reduce venous congestion, and a shoulder roll placed to hyperextend the neck.
subTopics.landmarks: • Surgical Landmarks: Suprasternal notch, thyroid cartilage, sternocleidomastoid muscles, and anterior border of the trapezius.

techniqueSteps.text: • Substernal Goiter Extension: Mobilization steps detailing finger dissection along the pretracheal fascia into the superior mediastinum.
• Tracheal Devitalisation Risk: Preserving the lateral blood supply to the tracheal cartilage during mobilization of large adherent lobes.
• Surgical Exposure and Incision: Low collar collar incision with creation of subplatysmal flaps to expose the thyroid gland.

redZone.name: Retrosternal Mobilization Danger
redZone.caution: • Mediastinal Hemorrhage: Retrosternal goiters must be mobilized by gentle finger dissection close to the thyroid capsule. Avoid blind tearing to prevent injury to the brachiocephalic vein or mediastinal arteries, which would necessitate sternotomy.

subTopics.closure: • Drain Placement: Consider placing a closed-suction active drain (e.g., Jackson-Pratt) in the thyroid bed if a large retrosternal cavity remains, to prevent hematoma formation. Close strap muscles and platysma in layers.
subTopics.extensions: • Postoperative Surveillance: Monitor TSH postoperatively. Hemithyroidectomy carries a 15-20% risk of hypothyroidism, while total thyroidectomy requires immediate lifelong Levothyroxine replacement.

subTopics.title: Thyroid Nodule
subTopics.summary: Clinical workup of the thyroid nodule. Detailed ACR TI-RADS risk classification, Bethesda cytopathology, and the clinical application of molecular markers.
indications: • TI-RADS Risk Stratification: Thyroid nodules are evaluated by high-frequency ultrasound. Points are assigned for Composition, Echogenicity, Shape (taller-than-wide), Margin, and Echogenic Foci to determine TI-RADS categories (TR1-TR5).
• FNA Size Criteria: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) is recommended for TR5 nodules >= 1.0 cm, TR4 nodules >= 1.5 cm, and TR3 nodules >= 2.5 cm. Purely cystic nodules (TR1) do not require biopsy.
• Bethesda Classification: Cytology reporting categorized from Bethesda I (nondiagnostic) to Bethesda VI (malignant), driving subsequent surgical or surveillance decisions.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Local Anesthesia: Local infiltration of the skin with 1% lidocaine for ultrasound-guided FNA. No sedation needed.
subTopics.positioning: • Biopsy Posture: Supine position with a pillow under the shoulders to hyperextend the neck, stabilizing the target area.
subTopics.landmarks: • Target Landmarks: Nodule borders identified under real-time ultrasound, with carotid artery and jugular vein mapped to avoid inadvertent puncture.

techniqueSteps.text: • ACR TI-RADS Calculator Grid: Point allocation mapping nodule sonographic characteristics to malignancy risk and FNA thresholds.
• Ultrasound-Guided FNA Technique: Real-time needle visualization using a parallel approach to ensure correct targeting of the nodule center.
• Bethesda Classification Decision Tree: Management pathways mapping Bethesda categories to molecular testing, surveillance, or surgical resection.

• Thyroid — Figure 4: Illustration depicting clinical concepts, diagnostic algorithms, or pathology features in Chapter 6.
    • Nevertheless more than: Nevertheless, more than two thirds of ETA members (Bennedbaek et al 2000) routinely use scintigraphy, while less than 25% of ATA members prefer such a strategy (Bennedbaek and Hegedus 2000; Bonnema et al.
    • Indisputable indications scintigraphy: Indisputable indications for scintigraphy, in the setting of a nodular goiter, are hyperthyroidism (to visualize hot nodules suitable for 131I therapy) or when ﬁne- needle aspiration (FNA) cytology has shown a follicular neoplasm, since hot nodules with great certainty are benign (Hegedus et al.
    • 3 Scintigraphy: 3 Scintigraphy of a thyroid with an autonomously functioning thyroid nodule (“hot” nodule) in the right lobe Fig 4 Scintigraphy of a thyroid with a “cold” thyroid nodule in the right lobe Thyroid Nodule.

redZone.name: Bethesda III and IV Indeterminacy
redZone.caution: • Diagnostic Lobectomy Avoidance: Indeterminate cytology (AUS/FLUS, FN/SFN) has a 10-30% malignancy risk. Do not proceed to immediate total thyroidectomy; perform molecular testing (GeneClassifier, ThyroSeq) to rule out malignancy and guide a targeted lobectomy decision.

subTopics.closure: • Biopsy Resolution: Apply direct pressure to the puncture site for 5-10 minutes to prevent hematoma. Apply sterile dressing.
subTopics.extensions: • Clinical Follow-Up: Benign cytology (Bethesda II) has a <3% false-negative rate and is managed with serial ultrasound surveillance in 12-24 months.

title: Thyroiditis
summary: Comprehensive guide to inflammatory disorders of the thyroid, including Hashimoto's thyroiditis, postpartum/silent thyroiditis, and acute/subacute thyroiditis.

subTopics.title: Hashimoto´s Thyroiditis
subTopics.summary: Hashimoto's chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis. Pathophysiology, diagnostic markers, long-term replacement, and the high-yield risk association with primary thyroid lymphoma.
indications: • Chronic Hypothyroidism: The most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions. Autoimmune destruction of thyrocytes leads to gradual thyroid failure and goitrous or atrophic changes.
• Autoantibody Markers: Highly elevated anti-TPO (90-95% sensitivity) and anti-Tg antibodies are diagnostic of chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis.
• Sonographic Features: Diffuse hypoechogenicity, coarse heterogeneous echotexture, and thin fibrotic bands crossing the parenchyma.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Surveillance: No anesthesia required. Biopsy for rapid enlargement is performed under local infiltration.
subTopics.positioning: • Clinical Examination: Comfortably seated position for palpation and ultrasound examination.
subTopics.landmarks: • Delphian Node Prominence: Prominent prelaryngeal lymph node (Delphian node) in the midline above the cricoid cartilage, often enlarged in autoimmune thyroiditis.

techniqueSteps.text: • Hashimoto's Histopathology Features: Intense lymphocytic infiltration with germinal center formation and Hurthle cell (oncocytic) metaplasia.
• Sonographic Parenchymal Changes: Ultrasound image showing diffuse heterogeneous hypoechogenicity and pseudolobulation.
• Lymphoma Diagnostic Algorithm: Workflow mapping rapid goiter growth to urgent ultrasound, core biopsy, and immunohistochemistry.

• Hashimoto´s — Figure 4: Fig. 3 Differentiation of CD4+ T cells. Indicated are cytokines that promote differentiation into one of the four T-cell subsets and the cytokines that are predominantly produced by each T-cell subset (Modiﬁed from Pyzik et al. 2015) Hashimoto’s Thyroiditis

    • vitro differentiation: In vitro differentiation of T lymphocytes into Th17 cells (induced by IL-23/IL-6) is also enhanced (Figueroa-Vega et al.

redZone.name: Rapid Enlargement and Primary Thyroid Lymphoma
redZone.caution: • Malignancy Risk: Patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis have a 60-80 fold increased risk of primary thyroid lymphoma. A rapidly enlarging thyroid mass in this cohort is lymphoma until proven otherwise. Do not perform thyroidectomy; diagnose via core/FNA biopsy as lymphoma is highly responsive to chemotherapy/radiation.

subTopics.closure: • Hormone Replacement: Initiate Levothyroxine at 1.6 mcg/kg/day to normalize TSH. Titrate dose every 6-8 weeks based on serum TSH checks.
subTopics.extensions: • Long-term Surveillance: Periodic ultrasound surveillance is required to monitor for suspicious nodules arising in a background of chronic inflammation.

subTopics.title: Postpartum Thyroiditis and Silent Thyroiditis
subTopics.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis (PPT) and silent (subacute lymphocytic) thyroiditis. Clinical phases, differential diagnostics, and treatment parameters.
indications: • Postpartum Thyroiditis: Autoimmune thyroiditis occurring within 12 months postpartum. Follows a classic triphasic pattern: transient thyrotoxicosis (1-3 months), transient hypothyroidism (3-6 months), and return to euthyroidism.
• Silent Thyroiditis: Presents with a similar triphasic pattern outside of the postpartum period, without thyroid tenderness or systemic symptoms.
• Diagnostic Scintigraphy: Extremely low radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) in the thyrotoxic phase, reflecting follicular destruction rather than hormone overproduction.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Outpatient Care: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Clinical Setup: Seated position for standard examination and phlebotomy.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroid Bed Anatomy: Euthyroid or minimally enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland on palpation.

techniqueSteps.text: • Triphasic Hormone Curve: Diagram mapping TSH, Free T4, and clinical phases over 12 months.
• RAIU Diagnostic Differentiation: Scintigraphy comparison showing absent uptake in silent thyroiditis vs. diffuse high uptake in Graves' disease.
• Clinical Postpartum Workflow: Screening recommendations and follow-up protocol for high-risk patients (type 1 diabetics, positive anti-TPO).

redZone.name: Contraindication of Antithyroid Drugs
redZone.caution: • Avoid Methimazole and RAI: The hyperthyroid phase in postpartum and silent thyroiditis is caused by release of pre-formed hormone from damaged follicles. Antithyroid drugs (Methimazole/PTU) and radioactive iodine are completely ineffective and contraindicated. Use Beta-blockers (propranolol) for symptom control.

subTopics.closure: • Hypothyroid Phase Management: Start Levothyroxine (50-100 mcg daily) during the hypothyroid phase if symptoms are severe. Re-evaluate for withdrawal at 12 months, as 80% recover thyroid function.
subTopics.extensions: • Chronic Hypothyroidism Risk: Approximately 20% of patients with postpartum thyroiditis remain permanently hypothyroid and require lifelong replacement.

subTopics.title: Acute and Subacute Thyroiditis
subTopics.summary: Pathogenesis and clinical differentiation of painful Subacute Granulomatous (de Quervain’s) thyroiditis and life-threatening Acute Suppurative (infectious) thyroiditis.
indications: • Subacute Granulomatous (de Quervain's): Painful, tender goiter following a viral upper respiratory infection. Accompanied by high ESR/CRP, fever, and transient thyrotoxicosis.
• Acute Suppurative Thyroiditis: Bacterial infection of the thyroid gland, presenting with high fever, unilateral fluctuant neck mass, and localized skin erythema.
• ESR & RAIU Markers: De Quervain's presents with ESR > 50-100 mm/h and low RAIU (<1-2% at 24 hours). Suppurative thyroiditis presents with leucocytosis and ultrasound fluctuancy.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Surgical Intervention: Suppurative thyroiditis abscess requires local infiltration or general anesthesia for surgical drainage.
subTopics.positioning: • Drainage Posture: Supine position with the neck extended for open or ultrasound-guided aspiration.
subTopics.landmarks: • Piriform Sinus Fistula: A tract originating from the left piriform sinus to the upper thyroid pole, the primary route of infection in recurrent childhood suppurative thyroiditis.

techniqueSteps.text: • de Quervain's Histology: Multicellular giant cell granulomas surrounding disrupted thyroid follicles.
• Piriform Sinus Fistula Tract: Diagnostic barium swallow depicting a fistula tract from the piriform recess to the left thyroid lobe.
• Abscess Drainage Technique: Incision and drainage of a suppurative thyroid lobe, demonstrating protection of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

• Acute — Figure 4: Fig. 3 (a) Pathological changes of subacute thyroiditis, showing characteristic features of granulomatous change with multinucleated giant cells (arrows) and interfollicular inﬁltration by mononuclear cells and lymphocytes. For comparison, pathological appearances of Hashimoto’s thyroiditis are shown (b), with follicular atrophy and extensive lymphocytic inﬁltration Acute and Subacute Thyroiditis
    • pathological ﬁndings include: pathological ﬁndings include, in the early stage, inﬁltration of thyroid follicles with mononuclear cells and lymphocytes and ultimately patchy disruption of the thyroid follicle epithelial lining, and loss of follicular integrity is seen.
    • Interfollicular ﬁbrosis may: Interfollicular ﬁbrosis may occur later, but eventually pathological changes return to normal with minimal residual thyroid ﬁbrosis seen after resolution of the disease (Engkakul et al.
    • Differential Diagnosis primary: Differential Diagnosis The primary differential diagnosis of a tender, inﬂamed thyroid with systemic symptoms is acute infectious thyroiditis, which is a more common condition in children than subacute thyroiditis.
    • Other causes painful: Other causes of painful anterior neck swelling and fever should also be considered (see Table 2).

redZone.name: Inadvertent Surgical Thyroidectomy
redZone.caution: • de Quervain's Misdiagnosis: Painful de Quervain's thyroiditis is a self-limiting inflammatory process. Do not perform thyroidectomy; treat with high-dose NSAIDs or a prednisone taper (40 mg daily) to resolve the pain and inflammation.

subTopics.closure: • Suppurative Resolution: Close abscess cavity over a corrugated or active drain. Obtain Gram stain and culture to guide targeted IV antibiotics.
subTopics.extensions: • Fistula Identification: Perform a barium swallow or direct laryngoscopy after the acute infection resolves to identify and excise a persistent piriform sinus fistula.

title: Hypothyroidism
summary: Pathogenesis, classifications, central/congenital hypothyroidism, and detailed guidelines for levothyroxine replacement and surveillance.

subTopics.title: Classification and Etiopathogenesis of Hypothyroidism
subTopics.summary: Etiology and clinical classification of hypothyroidism, including primary, secondary (central), and congenital manifestations.
indications: • Primary Hypothyroidism: Direct thyroid gland failure (Hashimoto's, post-thyroidectomy, post-radioiodine, iodine deficiency). Characterized by elevated TSH and low Free T4.
• Secondary & Central Hypothyroidism: Pituitary or hypothalamic failure (pituitary tumor, Sheehan's syndrome, radiation). Characterized by low or inappropriately normal TSH and low Free T4.
• Clinical Presentation: Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, constipation, and delayed relaxation of deep tendon reflexes.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Therapy: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Clinical Setup: Seated posture for clinical examination and standard venous blood collection.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroid Bed Assessment: Palpable goiter vs. atrophic, non-palpable gland.

techniqueSteps.text: • Classification Schema: Diagnostic table separating primary, secondary, tertiary, and resistance syndromes by TSH, FT4, and TRH stimulation testing.
• Myxedema Pathophysiology: Accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in interstitial spaces, leading to non-pitting edema and macroglossia.
• Pituitary Stalk Compression: MRI brain scan detailing pituitary adenoma compressing the stalk, leading to central thyroid failure.

redZone.name: Pre-treatment Adrenal Crisis Danger
redZone.caution: • Rule Out ACTH Defect: In patients with suspected central hypothyroidism, never administer Levothyroxine before evaluating the adrenal axis. Replacing thyroid hormone in untreated adrenal insufficiency increases cortisol clearance, precipitating a fatal adrenal crisis.

subTopics.closure: • Hormone Replacement: Initiate Levothyroxine. In patients with co-existing adrenal insufficiency, initiate glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone) prior to starting Levothyroxine.
subTopics.extensions: • TSH Surveillance: Target TSH range is 0.45 to 2.5 mIU/L in primary hypothyroidism. TSH cannot be used to monitor central hypothyroidism.

subTopics.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism
subTopics.summary: Congenital hypothyroidism (cretinism). Pathophysiology, neonatal screening protocols, and emergency levothyroxine initiation guidelines.
indications: • Thyroid Dysgenesis: Structural defects in gland development (athyreosis, ectopy, hypoplasia), accounting for 80-85% of cases. Ectopic thyroid tissue is the most common variant.
• Thyroid Dyshormonogenesis: Inborn errors of thyroid hormone synthesis (TPO defects, NIS mutations, Pendrin defects), inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
• Clinical Signs: Prolonged neonatal jaundice, poor feeding, lethargy, macroglossia, umbilical hernia, wide posterior fontanelle, and hypotonia.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Neonatal Evaluation: No anesthesia or sedation required for screening or diagnostic imaging.
subTopics.positioning: • Blood Draw and Imaging: Supine neonate stabilized for heel-prick blood draw or neck ultrasound.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroglossal Duct Tract: Anatomical pathway from the foramen caecum at the base of the tongue to the lower neck, the site of ectopic thyroid tissue.

techniqueSteps.text: • Congenital Screening Protocol: Heel-prick filter paper blood spot collection at 24-48 hours of life, followed by rapid TSH assay.
• Lingual Thyroid Ectopia: Scintigraphy scan using Tc-99m detailing tracer concentration at the base of the tongue with an empty thyroid bed.

redZone.name: Neonatal Brain Development Emergency
redZone.caution: • Delay and Permanent Cretinism: Severe congenital hypothyroidism leads to irreversible neurological damage if untreated. Initiate Levothyroxine (10-15 mcg/kg/day) immediately upon confirmation, ideally within the first 14 days of life. Do not delay treatment for imaging.

subTopics.closure: • Hormone Administration: Crushed levothyroxine tablets suspended in breast milk or water. Avoid administration with soy formula or iron, which impair absorption.
subTopics.extensions: • Pediatric Surveillance: Monitor TSH and Free T4 weekly in the first month, then every 2-4 weeks until 6 months. Maintain Free T4 in the upper half of the normal range.

subTopics.title: Central Hypothyroidism
subTopics.summary: Central hypothyroidism. Evaluation of secondary (pituitary) and tertiary (hypothalamic) thyroid hormone deficiency, sella imaging, and hormone replacement titration.
indications: • Low/Normal TSH with Low FT4: Diagnostic hallmark of central hypothyroidism. Pituitary or hypothalamic pathology leads to the secretion of biologically inactive TSH or absent TSH.
• Pituitary Stalk/Sella Pathology: Pituitary macroadenomas, craniopharyngiomas, infiltrative diseases (sarcoidosis, histiocytosis), Sheehan's syndrome, or traumatic brain injury.
• Panhypopituitarism: Often associated with ACTH, GH, Prolactin, and FSH/LH deficiencies.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Non-Surgical Care: No anesthesia required. Pituitary resection is performed under general anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Brain MRI Scan: Supine position in the MRI scanner.
subTopics.landmarks: • Sellar Anatomy: Sella turcica, optic chiasm, cavernous sinus, sphenoid sinus, and pituitary gland.

techniqueSteps.text: • Pituitary Tumor Compression: MRI brain scan detailing a pituitary macroadenoma compressing normal pituitary tissue and the pituitary stalk.

redZone.name: Pre-Replacement Adrenal Crisis Warning
redZone.caution: • L-Thyroxine and Adrenal Insufficiency: Replacing thyroid hormone increases metabolic rate and hepatic clearance of cortisol. If a co-existing ACTH deficiency is present, starting Levothyroxine before glucocorticoid replacement will precipitate an acute adrenal crisis.

subTopics.closure: • Hormone Replacement Order: Initiate Hydrocortisone (15-20 mg daily in divided doses) first, then start Levothyroxine (1.6 mcg/kg/day) 24-48 hours later.
subTopics.extensions: • Monitoring Parameters: Serum TSH is useless for monitoring central hypothyroidism. Titrate Levothyroxine dose based solely on keeping Free T4 in the upper half of the normal reference range.

subTopics.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hypothyroidism
subTopics.summary: Management guidelines for primary hypothyroidism. Levothyroxine dosing, absorption barriers, pregnancy adjustments, and subclinical hypothyroidism indications.
indications: • Levothyroxine Replacement: Lifelong replacement therapy for primary hypothyroidism. Standard starting dose is 1.6 mcg/kg/day based on ideal body weight.

• Cardiovascular Start-Low Dosing: Elderly patients or those with active coronary artery disease should start at 12.5-25 mcg/day to prevent precipitating angina, myocardial infarction, or arrhythmias.
• Subclinical Hypothyroidism Treatment: Indicated if TSH > 10 mIU/L, positive anti-TPO antibodies, pregnancy, or in patients with symptoms of hypothyroidism.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Outpatient Management: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Venous Blood Draw: Seated posture for routine TSH monitoring.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroid Bed Status: Non-tender euthyroid or atrophic gland.

techniqueSteps.text: • Levothyroxine Dosing Nomogram: Calculation table adjusting starting dose based on age, weight, and cardiovascular status.
• Intestinal Absorption Barriers: Diagram showing mechanisms of drug interactions (calcium, iron, PPIs) on gastric acidity and drug bioavailability.

redZone.name: Myxedema Coma and Severe Hypothyroidism
redZone.caution: • Life-Threatening Myxedema: Severe, untreated hypothyroidism precipitated by infection, cold exposure, or sedation. Presents with hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, and altered mental status. Treat with IV levothyroxine loading (300-500 mcg) and IV hydrocortisone.

subTopics.closure: • Hormone Administration: Take Levothyroxine on an empty stomach with water 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Avoid concurrent use of calcium, iron, or proton pump inhibitors, which impair absorption.
subTopics.extensions: • TSH Monitoring: Recheck TSH 6-8 weeks after starting therapy or changing the dose. Once stable, monitor TSH annually.

title: Hyperthyroidism and Thyrotoxicosis
summary: Management of Graves' disease, Graves' ophthalmopathy, antithyroid drug therapy, radioiodine ablation, toxic adenoma, and toxic multinodular goiter (MNG).

subTopics.title: Graves´ Disease
subTopics.summary: Graves' disease. Pathophysiology, diagnostic markers, hypervascularity imaging, and first-line medical therapy with antithyroid drugs.
indications: • Autoimmune Hyperthyroidism: Autoantibodies (TSI/TRAb) stimulate the TSH receptor, leading to diffuse toxic goiter, hyperthyroidism, dermopathy (pretibial myxedema), and acropachy.
• Clinical Presentation: Weight loss, heat intolerance, tremors, palpitations, anxiety, diffuse goiter with bruit, and orbitopathy.
• Biochemical Profile: Suppressed TSH (<0.01 mIU/L) with elevated Free T4 and Free T3, and positive TSI/TRAb antibodies.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Control: Outpatient therapy requires no anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Phlebotomy & Imaging: Seated position for blood draw; supine with neck extended for thyroid ultrasound.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroid Hypervascularity: Marked increase in parenchymal blood flow on color Doppler, known as the 'thyroid inferno'.

techniqueSteps.text: • TSH Receptor Antibody Binding: Diagram showing TSI autoantibodies binding the TSH receptor and activating cyclic AMP pathways.
• Thyroid Inferno on Doppler: Ultrasound Doppler scan detailing hypervascular flow pattern in untreated Graves' disease.
• Antithyroid Drug Action: Mechanisms of Methimazole and PTU inhibiting thyroid peroxidase, and PTU blocking peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion.

Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) acts as a cell surface homodimer and has a large myeloperoxidase (MPO)-like domain, with 2 smaller, complement control protein (CCP)-like and EGF-like domains (not shown). There are speciﬁc TPO epitopes that are predominantly recognized by autoantibodies in human autoimmune thyroid diseases (Chazenbalk et al. 1993).     • Interestingly these patterns: Interestingly, these patterns of TPO antibody response (epitopic ﬁngerprints) are stable, persisting for many years; they are also inherited in a dominant fashion (Jaume et al.

    • presence: In the presence of clear extrathyroidal signs of Graves’ disease (e.g., proptosis, dermopathy), no further testing beyond free thyroid hormone(s) and TSH is neces- sary.
    • absence: In the absence of these features, an attempt to secure an etiological diagnosis should be made.
    • gold-standard test: The gold-standard test is a highly sensitive TSHR-stimulating serum antibody tests, including indirect assay of TSH-stimulating antibodies by TSH-binding inhibitory immunoglobulin (TBI or TBII) or TPO antibody assay, are commonly employed and have >90% sensitivity for Graves’ disease.
    • absence: In the absence of a positive antibody test, or in the presence of a nodular thyroid on palpation, the thyroid gland should be imaged.

redZone.name: Antithyroid Drug Hepatotoxicity
redZone.caution: • Liver Failure Risk: Propylthiouracil (PTU) can cause severe, drug-induced liver failure, whereas Methimazole (MMI) is associated with cholestatic jaundice. Check liver function tests (LFTs) at baseline. PTU is restricted to the first trimester of pregnancy and thyroid storm.

subTopics.closure: • Antithyroid Dosing: Start Methimazole at 10-30 mg daily. Monitor Free T4 and Free T3 in 4-6 weeks; do not rely on TSH, which remains suppressed.
subTopics.extensions: • Therapy Duration: Continue antithyroid drug therapy for 12-18 months. Remission rate after withdrawal is approximately 30-40%.

subTopics.title: Graves´ Ophthalmopathy
subTopics.summary: Graves' ophthalmopathy. Pathophysiology, CAS (Clinical Activity Score), orbital imaging, and therapeutic steroid and surgical decompression guidelines.
indications: • Thyroid Eye Disease: Autoimmune inflammatory disorder of the orbit, mediated by TSH receptor and IGF-1 receptor cross-talk in orbital fibroblasts, causing glycosaminoglycan accumulation and extraocular muscle hypertrophy.
• Clinical Presentation: Proptosis, lid retraction (Dalrymple's sign), lid lag (von Graefe's sign), diplopia, orbital pain, and optic neuropathy.
• Etiological Risk Factors: Smoking increases the risk of developing or worsening ophthalmopathy by 7-8 fold.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Orbital Decompression: Performed under general anesthesia. Optic nerve checks are critical postoperatively.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Decompression: Supine position with the head of the bed elevated 30 degrees to reduce orbital venous pressure.
subTopics.landmarks: • Orbital Walls: Medial wall (lamina papyracea), lateral wall, inferior wall (orbital floor), and optic canal.

techniqueSteps.text: • Extraocular Muscle Hypertrophy: Orbital CT scan detailing bilateral enlargement of extraocular muscle bellies (sparing the tendons).
• Orbital Decompression Technique: Transantral or transcaruncular approach to decompress the medial orbital wall and orbital floor.
• Clinical Activity Score (CAS): Evaluation grid mapping signs like conjunctival injection, chemosis, and lid edema to active disease.

    • Lagophthalmos increases risk: Lagophthalmos increases the risk of corneal lesions, particularly if Bell’s phenomenon (upward rotation of the eye upon eyelid closure) is poor.

redZone.name: Optic Nerve Compression Emergency
redZone.caution: • Threatened Vision: Severe orbital congestion causing visual acuity loss, color vision deficits, or optic disc edema. Treat immediately with high-dose IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy (500 mg weekly). Proceed to emergency surgical orbital decompression if refractory.

subTopics.closure: • Decompression Resolution: Excise the medial and/or lateral orbital walls and retrobulbar fat to allow the orbital contents to expand, resolving optic nerve compression.
subTopics.extensions: • Radioactive Iodine Prophylaxis: RAI therapy can worsen active Graves' ophthalmopathy. If RAI is used, concurrent steroid prophylaxis (prednisone taper starting at 0.3-0.5 mg/kg/day) is mandatory.

subTopics.title: Treatment of Graves´ Disease
subTopics.summary: Comparison of definitive therapies for Graves' disease: Antithyroid drugs, Radioiodine (I-131) ablation, and Total Thyroidectomy.
indications: • Antithyroid Drugs: First-line for patients seeking a non-destructive option, mild disease, or prior to definitive surgery or radioiodine therapy.
• Radioiodine (I-131) Ablation: Preferred definitive therapy in the US for patients with significant hyperthyroidism, toxic adenoma, or recurrent disease who wish to avoid surgery.

• Total Thyroidectomy: Indicated for patients with large goiters (>80g), compressive symptoms, co-existing suspicious nodules, active moderate-to-severe Graves' ophthalmopathy, or in patients wishing to avoid radiation.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Total Thyroidectomy: General endotracheal anesthesia. Use of a NIM (Nerve Integrity Monitor) endotracheal tube is standard for recurrent laryngeal nerve monitoring.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Positioning: Supine position with head elevated 15 degrees and neck hyperextended (Rosser position) to optimize access to the thyroid gland.
subTopics.landmarks: • Thyroidectomy Landmarks: Midline raphe of strap muscles, thyroid capsule, cricoid cartilage, recurrent laryngeal nerve, superior laryngeal nerve, and parathyroid glands.

techniqueSteps.text: • Surgical Access collar Incision: Low collar collar incision positioned 2 cm above the sternoclavicular joints along a natural skin crease.

redZone.name: Thyroid Storm Prevention
redZone.caution: • Preoperative Preparation Failure: Never perform thyroidectomy on a thyrotoxic patient, as the surgical stress can trigger a fatal thyroid storm. Achieve euthyroidism preoperatively using antithyroid drugs, and add Lugol's iodine solution (5-7 drops tid for 10 days pre-op) to decrease vascularity.

subTopics.closure: • Strap Muscle Closure: Reapproximate the strap muscles in the midline. Close the platysma layer and perform a subcuticular skin closure. Place a drain if a large dead space exists.
subTopics.extensions: • Lifelong Replacement: Total thyroidectomy results in permanent hypothyroidism. Initiate Levothyroxine at 1.6 mcg/kg/day postoperatively. Monitor serum calcium and PTH levels.

subTopics.title: Toxic Adenoma and Multinodular Toxic Goiter
subTopics.summary: Toxic adenoma and toxic multinodular goiter (Plummer's disease). Diagnostic parameters, radionuclide uptake imaging, and comparative management protocols.
indications: • Radionuclide Scan Profiles: Scintigraphy shows a single hot nodule with suppression of the rest of the gland (toxic adenoma) or multiple areas of high uptake (toxic MNG).
• Clinical Presentation: Hyperthyroidism in older adults, often presenting with tachyarrhythmias (atrial fibrillation), weight loss, muscle weakness, or bone loss.
• Biochemical Markers: Suppressed TSH, elevated Free T3 (often elevated out of proportion to Free T4), and negative TSI/TRAb antibodies.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Surgical Lobectomy/Thyroidectomy: General endotracheal anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Posture: Supine position with head elevated 15 degrees and neck extended (Rosser position).
subTopics.landmarks: • Nodule Anatomical Borders: Internal jugular vein, carotid artery, cricoid cartilage, and recurrent laryngeal nerve.

techniqueSteps.text: • Radionuclide Uptake Patterns: Scintigraphy comparison detailing toxic adenoma (single hot nodule, suppressed gland) vs. toxic MNG (patchy nodular uptake).
• Thyroid Lobectomy Technique: Mobilization and resection of a single hyperfunctioning lobe, demonstrating preservation of the contralateral lobe.
• Thyrotoxic AF Management: Cardiovascular management protocol combining beta-blockers and anticoagulation during hyperthyroid control.

redZone.name: Cardiac Arrhythmia Danger
redZone.caution: • Thyrotoxic Atrial Fibrillation: Thyrotoxicosis in elderly patients with multinodular goiters carries a high risk of atrial fibrillation and cardiovascular collapse. Optimize thyroid status preoperatively using Methimazole and beta-blockers (propranolol).

subTopics.closure: • Lobectomy/Thyroidectomy Resolution: Ligate the middle thyroid veins, mobilize the thyroid lobes, identify and protect the RLN, and resect the target tissue (lobectomy for toxic adenoma; total thyroidectomy for toxic MNG).
subTopics.extensions: • Lifelong Levothyroxine Replacement: Required post-total thyroidectomy. Hemithyroidectomy for toxic adenoma preserves euthyroid status in 80% of patients.

title: Thyroid Carcinoma
summary: Molecular pathogenesis, surgical approach to differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), medullary thyroid cancer (MTC), anaplastic thyroid cancer (ATC), and tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) targeted medical therapies.

subTopics.title: Pathogenesis of Thyroid Carcinoma
subTopics.summary: Pathogenesis and genetic drivers of thyroid carcinoma. Molecular pathways in Papillary (BRAF, RET/PTC), Follicular (RAS, PAX8-PPARG), Medullary (RET proto-oncogene), and Anaplastic (TP53) cancers.
indications: • Papillary Thyroid Cancer (PTC): Most common thyroid cancer (80-85%). Driven by BRAF V600E mutations (associated with aggressive biology) or RET/PTC rearrangements. Characterized by lymphatic spread.

• Follicular Thyroid Cancer (FTC): Driven by RAS mutations or PAX8-PPARG fusions. Spreads hematogenously to bone and lungs. Diagnosis requires capsular or vascular invasion, which cannot be determined on FNA.
• Medullary Thyroid Cancer (MTC): Neuroendocrine tumor of parafollicular C-cells. Driven by RET proto-oncogene mutations. Can be familial (MEN 2A/2B, FMTC) or sporadic.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Surgical Thyroidectomy: General endotracheal anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Posture: Supine position with head elevated 15 degrees and neck extended (Rosser position).
subTopics.landmarks: • C-cell Anatomical Location: Parafollicular C-cells are located primarily in the upper two-thirds of the thyroid lobes, the origin of medullary cancer.

techniqueSteps.text: • Genetic Mutational Pathways: Diagram mapping BRAF, RAS, RET, and TP53 mutations to differentiated, medullary, and anaplastic thyroid cancers.
• Capsular Invasion Criteria: Histopathology slide showing follicular cells breaking through the fibrous capsule, the hallmark of Follicular Carcinoma.

redZone.name: Co-existing Pheochromocytoma Warning
redZone.caution: • MEN 2 Diagnostic Priority: Prior to performing thyroidectomy in any patient with Medullary Thyroid Cancer, always screen for a co-existing Pheochromocytoma (plasma fractionated metanephrines). If present, the pheochromocytoma must be resected first to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis.

subTopics.closure: • Total Thyroidectomy: Perform total thyroidectomy. Complete central neck dissection (Level VI) is standard for medullary cancer.
subTopics.extensions: • RET Mutational Analysis: Germline RET testing is mandatory for all patients with MTC to identify syndromic disease and guide prophylactic thyroidectomy in first-degree relatives.

subTopics.title: Differentiated Thyroid Carcinoma of Follicular Origin
subTopics.summary: Surgical management of differentiated thyroid carcinoma (papillary and follicular). ATA risk stratification, lobectomy vs. total thyroidectomy, and radioactive iodine remnant ablation.
indications: • Thyroid Lobectomy vs. Total Thyroidectomy: Lobectomy is indicated for low-risk tumors < 4 cm, no extrathyroidal extension, and no clinically positive lymph nodes. Total thyroidectomy is indicated for tumors >= 4 cm, extrathyroidal extension, or positive nodes.

• ATA Risk Stratification: Classified into Low (unifocal, no vascular invasion), Intermediate (microscopic extrathyroidal extension, clinical nodes), and High risk (macroscopic invasion, distant metastasis).
• Lymph Node Dissection: Therapeutic central neck dissection (Level VI) is indicated for clinically positive central nodes. Prophylactic central dissection may be considered for T3/T4 tumors.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Total Thyroidectomy & Neck Dissection: General endotracheal anesthesia. Use of a NIM tube is standard for recurrent laryngeal nerve monitoring.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Setup: Supine position with head elevated 15 degrees and neck hyperextended (Rosser position) to maximize exposure.
subTopics.landmarks: • Surgical Anatomy Landmarks: Recurrent laryngeal nerve, superior laryngeal nerve external branch, Zuckerkandl's tubercle, ligament of Berry, and parathyroid glands.

techniqueSteps.text: • Surgical Exposure of Thyroid Lobe: Low collar collar incision with creation of subplatysmal flaps to expose the thyroid gland.
• Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Dissection: Dissection and visualization of the RLN in the tracheoesophageal groove, lateral to Berry's ligament.
• Parathyroid Gland Preservation: Identifying and dissecting the superior parathyroid gland off the posterior aspect of the thyroid lobe, preserving its blood supply.

redZone.name: Bilateral Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury
redZone.caution: • Airway Obstruction: Bilateral injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerves results in vocal cord paralysis in the paramedian position, causing immediate airway obstruction postoperatively. This represents an emergency requiring re-intubation or tracheostomy.

subTopics.closure: • Wound Closure: Place a closed-suction drain if a neck dissection was performed. Reapproximate strap muscles, close the platysma layer, and perform a subcuticular skin closure.
subTopics.extensions: • Postoperative Radioactive Iodine (RAI): I-131 ablation is indicated for High-risk patients, and considered for Intermediate-risk patients to ablate normal thyroid tissue and facilitate thyroglobulin monitoring.

subTopics.title: Medullary Carcinoma
subTopics.summary: Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC). Diagnostic evaluation (calcitonin/CEA), RET mutation screening, total thyroidectomy, and compartment-oriented neck dissection.
indications: • C-cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma: Medullary thyroid cancer is a neuroendocrine tumor of parafollicular C-cells, characterized by calcitonin and CEA secretion. Calcitonin levels correlate with tumor burden.
• Surgical Indications: Total thyroidectomy is indicated for all biopsy-proven medullary thyroid cancers.
• Lymph Node Clearance: Central neck dissection (Level VI) is standard. Ipsilateral lateral neck dissection (Levels II-V) is indicated if the primary tumor is > 1.5 cm or central nodes are positive.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Total Thyroidectomy & Neck Dissection: General endotracheal anesthesia. Use of a NIM tube is standard for recurrent laryngeal nerve monitoring.
subTopics.positioning: • Surgical Setup: Supine position with head elevated 15 degrees and neck hyperextended (Rosser position) to maximize exposure.
subTopics.landmarks: • Surgical Anatomy Landmarks: Recurrent laryngeal nerve, superior laryngeal nerve, carotid sheath, and parathyroid glands.

techniqueSteps.text: • Central Neck Dissection: Bilateral dissection of Level VI lymph nodes, showing clearing of nodes along the trachea and recurrent laryngeal nerves.
• Lateral Neck Dissection: Dissection of Levels II-V lymph nodes, demonstrating identification and protection of the accessory nerve.
• RET Mutational Analysis: Diagnostic algorithm mapping RET mutation risk categories to prophylactic thyroidectomy timing in children.
• Pheochromocytoma Clearance Protocol: Preoperative clearance steps combining metanephrine measurements and alpha-blockade.

redZone.name: Rule Out Pheochromocytoma First
redZone.caution: • Hypertensive Crisis Danger: Prior to performing thyroidectomy in any patient with Medullary Thyroid Cancer, always screen for a co-existing Pheochromocytoma (plasma fractionated metanephrines). If present, the pheochromocytoma must be resected first to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis.

subTopics.closure: • Wound Closure: Place a closed-suction drain if a neck dissection was performed. Reapproximate strap muscles, close the platysma layer, and perform a subcuticular skin closure.
subTopics.extensions: • Postoperative Calcitonin/CEA: Monitor serum calcitonin and CEA levels postoperatively. A calcitonin doubling time of < 6 months indicates aggressive disease. MTC is not responsive to radioactive iodine.

subTopics.title: Anaplastic and Other Forms of Thyroid Carcinoma
subTopics.summary: Anaplastic thyroid cancer (ATC). Rapid diagnostic biopsy, airway management, and the clinical application of targeted therapies (BRAF inhibitors).
indications: • Aggressive Thyroid mass: Anaplastic thyroid cancer is an extremely aggressive, rapidly growing neck mass causing airway compromise, dysphagia, and neck pain.
• Airway Assessment: Immediate airway evaluation is mandatory, as progressive compression or invasion of the trachea can cause airway obstruction.
• Biopsy Confirmation: Core needle biopsy or incisional biopsy is required for rapid histological diagnosis and molecular profiling (BRAF V600E).
subTopics.anesthesia: • Airway Management & Biopsy: Core needle biopsy is performed under local anesthesia. Tracheostomy or surgical resection requires general endotracheal anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Airway Posture: Seated or semi-Fowler position to reduce airway compromise and venous pressure.
subTopics.landmarks: • Tracheal Landmarks: Cricoid cartilage, tracheal rings, and thyroid isthmus.

techniqueSteps.text: • Airway Compression Scans: CT neck scan detailing a massive anaplastic thyroid tumor compressing and displacing the trachea.

redZone.name: Debulking Surgery Contraindication
redZone.caution: • Avoid Non-Curative Resection: Debulking surgery for unresectable anaplastic thyroid cancer does not improve survival, delays systemic therapy, and carries high risk of airway catastrophe or carotid blowout. Surgery is only indicated if the tumor is localized and resectable with R0/R1 margins.

subTopics.closure: • Airway Protection: Tracheostomy is indicated for acute airway obstruction. Secure the tracheostomy tube carefully, as the tumor can displace the airway.
subTopics.extensions: • Targeted Therapy: For BRAF V600E mutated tumors, initiate targeted therapy with Dabrafenib and Trametinib immediately. For BRAF wild-type tumors, treat with external beam radiation and chemotherapy.

subTopics.title: New (Medical) Treatment for Thyroid Carcinoma
subTopics.summary: Targeted therapies for advanced, radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) and medullary thyroid cancer (MTC). Comparative TKI protocols.
indications: • RAI-Refractory DTC: Multi-kinase inhibitors (Lenvatinib, Sorafenib) are indicated for progressive, symptomatic, or locally advanced RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer.

• Advanced Medullary Cancer: RET-specific inhibitors (Selpercatinib, Pralsetinib) or multi-kinase inhibitors (Vandetanib, Cabozantinib) are indicated for progressive or symptomatic advanced medullary thyroid cancer.
• Molecular Profile: DNA sequencing of tumor tissue is critical to identify targetable mutations (RET, BRAF, NTRK fusion) and guide therapy.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Therapy: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Outpatient Dosing: Oral administration of targeted agents.
subTopics.landmarks: • Targeted Kinase Pathways: VEGFR, RET, BRAF, FGFR, PDGFR, and NTRK receptors.

techniqueSteps.text: • TKI Receptor Binding: Diagram showing Lenvatinib and Sorafenib binding the ATP pocket of tyrosine kinase receptors.
• RET Kinase Inhibition: Diagram mapping Selpercatinib and Pralsetinib targeted inhibition of RET-mutated kinase signaling.
• TKI Side Effect Management: Protocol for managing TKI-induced hypertension, proteinuria, and skin toxicity.

redZone.name: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Toxicity
redZone.caution: • Severe Adverse Events: TKIs are associated with severe cardiovascular toxicity, including arterial thromboembolism, myocardial infarction, QTc prolongation, and severe hypertension. Monitor EKG and blood pressure closely.

subTopics.closure: • Dose Titration: Adjust TKI dose based on tolerability and side effects. Manage adverse events (hypertension, proteinuria, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) aggressively.
subTopics.extensions: • Monitoring Parameters: Monitor tumor markers (thyroglobulin, calcitonin, CEA) and perform surveillance imaging (CT chest/abdomen) every 3-6 months.

title: Other Conditions and Drug Influences
summary: Thyroid diseases in pregnancy, non-thyroidal illness syndrome (NTIS) in critical illness, and drugs inducing thyroid dysfunction (amiodarone, lithium, TKIs, and checkpoint inhibitors).

subTopics.title: Thyroid Physiology and Thyroid Diseases in Pregnancy
subTopics.summary: Thyroid physiology during pregnancy, gestational-specific TSH ranges, and medical management of gestational thyrotoxicosis and hypothyroidism.
indications: • Pregnancy Physiology: High hCG stimulates TSH receptors, and estrogen increases Thyroid Binding Globulin (TBG). This leads to elevated Total T4/T3, but normal Free T4/T3.
• Gestational-Specific TSH Ranges: TSH reference ranges are lower during pregnancy. TSH should be interpreted using trimester-specific ranges (usually reduced by 0.4 mIU/L).
• Hypothyroidism Treatment: Levothyroxine dose must be increased by 30-50% as soon as pregnancy is confirmed, as thyroid hormone requirements increase in the first trimester.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Management: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • Venous Access: Seated posture for routine TSH monitoring.
subTopics.landmarks: • Physiological Goiter: Symmetrical enlargement of the thyroid gland due to increased clearance of iodine during pregnancy.

techniqueSteps.text: • Pregnancy Hormone Fluctuations: Graph mapping hCG, TSH, TBG, and thyroid hormone levels during pregnancy.
• hCG Thyroid Stimulation: Diagram showing the homology between hCG and TSH beta-subunits, explaining hCG thyroid stimulation.
• Fetal Embryopathy Risks: Comparative table detailing fetal risks associated with Methimazole vs. PTU.

redZone.name: Maternal Hyperthyroidism Control
redZone.caution: • Avoid Methimazole in First Trimester: Methimazole is associated with fetal embryopathy (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia). Use Propylthiouracil (PTU) in the first trimester, and switch to Methimazole in the second trimester.

subTopics.closure: • Dose Titration: Monitor TSH and Free T4 every 4 weeks. Target TSH range is < 2.5 mIU/L during pregnancy.
subTopics.extensions: • Postpartum Dosing: Postoperatively or postpartum, reduce Levothyroxine dose to pre-pregnancy levels. Recheck TSH in 6 weeks.

subTopics.title: Non-thyroidal Illness
subTopics.summary: Non-thyroidal illness syndrome (euthyroid sick syndrome) in critically ill patients. Diagnostic parameters, rT3 profiling, and treatment parameters.
indications: • Euthyroid Sick Syndrome: Alterations in thyroid hormone levels in critically ill patients (sepsis, trauma, cardiac surgery) without pre-existing thyroid disease.
• Hormonal Profile: Characterized by low T3, elevated Reverse T3 (rT3), normal or low T4, and normal or low TSH.
• Diagnostic Differentiation: Distinguish from central hypothyroidism (rT3 is high in euthyroid sick, low in pituitary disease).
subTopics.anesthesia: • ICU Care: No anesthesia required.
subTopics.positioning: • ICU Setup: Patient stabilized in the ICU.
subTopics.landmarks: • Systemic Inflammation: Elevated cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha) inhibit D1 deiodinase activity, preventing T4 to T3 conversion.

redZone.name: Avoid Thyroid Hormone Replacement
redZone.caution: • Do Not Treat: Do not treat euthyroid sick syndrome with Levothyroxine or T3 replacement. Therapy does not improve outcomes, and replacing thyroid hormones can precipitate cardiac ischemia or tachyarrhythmias.

subTopics.closure: • Diagnostic Re-evaluation: Recheck thyroid function tests after resolution of the acute illness. Thyroid hormone levels should return to normal.
subTopics.extensions: • TSH lag-time: Following recovery, TSH may rise transiently above the normal range. Re-evaluate in 4-6 weeks to rule out primary hypothyroidism.

subTopics.title: Drugs and Other Substances Interfering with Thyroid Function
subTopics.summary: Substances and medications interfering with thyroid function. Diagnostic and management protocols for Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis (AIT I & II).
indications: • Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis (AIT): Amiodarone contains high iodine content (37% by weight). Divided into AIT Type I (iodine-induced hyper-synthesis in underlying goiter) and AIT Type II (destructive thyroiditis).
• Lithium-Induced Hypothyroidism: Lithium concentrates in the thyroid gland and inhibits thyroid hormone release. Presents with goiter and hypothyroidism.
• Checkpoint Inhibitor Thyroiditis: Immunotherapy (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) can trigger a destructive thyroiditis, presenting with transient thyrotoxicosis followed by permanent hypothyroidism.
subTopics.anesthesia: • Medical Control: Outpatient therapy requires no anesthesia.
subTopics.positioning: • Clinical Setup: Seated posture for routine TSH monitoring and blood draws.
subTopics.landmarks: • Drug-Target Interactions: Follicular thyrocytes and immune checkpoint receptors.

techniqueSteps.text: • Amiodarone Radionuclide Scintigraphy: Scintigraphy scan detailing absent uptake in AIT Type II vs. patchy uptake in AIT Type I.

redZone.name: AIT Differentiation and Management
redZone.caution: • AIT Diagnostic Priority: Differentiating AIT Type I from Type II is critical. AIT Type I is treated with high-dose antithyroid drugs (Methimazole), whereas AIT Type II requires glucocorticoids (Prednisone). RAIU is normal/high in Type I, low/absent in Type II.

subTopics.closure: • Amiodarone-Induced Hypothyroidism (AIH): Treat AIH with Levothyroxine. Amiodarone does not need to be discontinued if thyroid hormone replacement is effective.
subTopics.extensions: • Baseline Screening: Obtain baseline TSH, Free T4, and Free T3 before starting Amiodarone, Lithium, or immune checkpoint inhibitors, and monitor periodically.
